medicinal_uses: Regarded as supremely sacred; planting and maintaining Tulasi in one's home is said to earn the reward of residence in Vaikuntha (the divine abode of Lord Vishnu) for twelve thousand years.

Planting Bilva confers the grace of Lord Siva and the blessings of goddess Laksmi, ensuring prosperity for future generations. Classified as a Jangala-adapted (arid-region) tree. Used as a component of Pancamula in treating Kapha diseases of plants. Fruit enhancement involves irrigation with a mixture of jaggery, ghee, milk, and honey to produce juicy, pulpy fruits in high yield.
dosage_preparation: Fruit-enhancement recipe: solution of guda (jaggery), ghee, milk, and honey applied as irrigation to bilva and kapittha trees (Verse 140).
classical_citations: Vrksayurveda of Surapala, Verses 1, 80, 140; Section 17 (Disease Treatment)

medicinal_uses: Planting Asvattha earns the graceful association of Lord Visnu upon departing the material world. Should be planted in the western direction of the house for auspicious results, but not in the east. Regarded as an indicator tree: its presence signals suitability for all types of crops. Yields the same spiritual merit as celibacy and land donation.

Planting grants spiritual merit equal to that of celibacy and land donation. Recommended for western directional planting near residences. A key ingredient of Triphala (along with Haritaki and Bibhitaka), widely used in plant disease treatment, fumigation, and bark-paste applications for fruit enhancement. Used in kapittha seed purification process.
dosage_preparation: Part of Triphala formulation applied to fruit trees via fumigation and irrigation. Bark-paste treatments for fruit-bearing trees.

medicinal_uses: Planting two Vata trees following classical procedure grants passage to Sivaloka. Should be planted in the eastern direction of the house. Classified as Anupa-adapted (marshy/riverine region). In horticultural practice, can be dwarfed by planting with four-pillar support and irrigating with milk-water. As an indicator tree, its presence signals suitability for cereal farming (barley and related crops).
dosage_preparation: Dwarfing treatment: support with four pillars (catussambha) and irrigate with milk-and-water mixture.

medicinal_uses: Planting three Nimba trees grants access to Suryaloka for three divine years. Part of the auspicious pentad (pancavrksa) of trees. Classified as Jangala-adapted (arid region). Leaf juice used as irrigation for treating other plants. In horticultural wonders, nimba seeds inserted into unripe kusmanda fruit, treated with ghee and honey, then extracted and sown to produce healthy neem fruits. As an indicator tree, its presence signals protection from famine.
dosage_preparation: Grafting method: nimba seed inserted into soft kusmanda fruit hole, sealed with ghee-honey paste, extracted after fruit ripens, sown in suitable land (Verse 267).

medicinal_uses: Planting four Plaksa trees earns the blessed merit of performing the rajasuyayajna. Should be planted in the northern direction of the house for auspicious results.

Planting five or six Amra trees ensures perpetual happiness and passage to Gandraloka. Classified as both Anupa-adapted and Sadharana-adapted (general region). For fruit production, roots are treated with a mixture of ankola fruit juice, ghee, honey, and pig fat dissolved in water, producing exceptionally sweet, large, and satisfying mangoes. In Dohada (plant-desire tradition), mango trees respond to a beautiful woman scratching the bark with her nails by sprouting tender leaves and buds. To protect fruiting mango trees from evil-sight, a broken earthen vessel is tied to the tree. As an indicator tree, its presence signals human happiness and welfare.
dosage_preparation: Fruit enhancement: roots treated with ankola juice, ghee, honey, and animal fat in water (Verse 126). Seed treatment: mango seeds treated with milk spray, seed paste of brhati, sesame, ash, and mustard, followed by cow-dung rubbing and fat fumigation for better viability (Verses 53-55).

medicinal_uses: Planting seven Palasa trees grants passage to Brahmaloka where one enjoys divine pleasure. Propagated by both seed and stem methods. Listed among restricted campus trees that should not be planted in residential compounds as they are not considered conducive to domestic comfort.

Planting eight Udumbara trees grants passage to Candraloka. Should be planted in the southern direction of the house. Its bark is used in preparing wound-healing paste for damaged plants along with other ingredients.
dosage_preparation: Wound paste: Udumbara bark combined with other plant barks, applied to tree wounds (Section 17).

medicinal_uses: Planting dadima ensures freedom from sorrow for seven births. Listed among restricted campus trees (not for house compound). Extensive fruit-enhancement protocols: fish, flesh, and buffalo milk mixture produces large, sweet fruits that cause branches to bend with weight. Triphala powder fumigation with ghee and fish-milk irrigation further enhances yield. Seeds sown for propagation; branch-layering with cow-dung manure and two-month irrigation also described. Evil-sight protection involves tying a skull to the fruiting tree. Grafted with karavira stems for unique modifications. Seeds treated with rooster blood, dried, and sown produce rapid-fruiting plants.
dosage_preparation: Fruit enhancement Recipe 1: fish flesh, nilalankha, buffalo milk mixed and applied (Verse 132). Recipe 2: ghee, honey, and fish paste on trunk with Triphala fumigation (Verse 134). Propagation: branch-layering with gomaya manure, irrigated for two months, incision at middle when leaves appear.

medicinal_uses: Planting kadali along with other specified trees ensures freedom from sorrow for seven births. Classified as Anupa-adapted (marshy region). Propagated by pasting roots with cow dung and planting in pits with adequate irrigation. Fruit enhanced with ash of rotted paddy husk and cow-dung manure applied to roots. In plant transformation lore, irrigating kadali with pig urine and sour substances reportedly produces pomegranate-like fruits. Banana leaves used in fumigation formulations to hasten flowering and fruiting of other trees. Fire transformation with heated gold needle on banana root converts the plant to a climber form. Kadali mandapa (banana-plant pavilion) described as a garden architectural element. As an indicator tree, presence signals availability of goats and sheep.
dosage_preparation: Propagation: root pasted with gomaya, planted in pits with ample irrigation (Verse on page 49). Fruit enhancement: ash of paddy husk and cow dung applied to roots (Verse 141). Transformation: irrigation with pig urine and sour liquid (Verse 238).

medicinal_uses: Planting panasa along with dadima, rambha, priyala, and rajadana prevents sorrow for seven births. Classified as Anupa-adapted (marshy region). Treated with Triphala decoction and paddy husk to produce larger, seedless fruits. Alternative recipe uses licorice, sesame, honey, and kumpa applied to roots for tastier fruits resembling bilva in size. Seeds treated with the standard milk-spray, paste, and fumigation protocol for enhanced viability.
dosage_preparation: Fruit enhancement: irrigate with Triphala decoction, cover with paddy husk for bigger seedless fruits (Verses 137-138). Root treatment: yastimadhu, tila, honey, and kumpa for improved taste (Verse 138). Seed viability: standard milk-paste-fumigation treatment.

medicinal_uses: Classified as Anupa-adapted (marshy/riverine region). Listed among trees receiving bark-paste treatment consisting of ghee, honey, ksara, lodhra powder, and barley powder for fruit enhancement, applied after scratching the bark appropriately.
dosage_preparation: Bark-paste treatment: ghee, honey, ksara, lodhra, and yava powder applied after bark scratching (Verse 139).

medicinal_uses: Classified as Anupa-adapted (marshy/coastal region). Multiple fruit-enhancement protocols described: irrigation with animal flesh cooked in water for narikela and allied palm trees; sesame-and-fish powder treatment; external application of salt, sesame, honey, and alcoholic liquids at nighttime for larger, heavier nuts; alkaline water with barley flour, paddy-husk water, and pulse soup for producing fruits of great size. The nutritive treatment with white mustard water, barley, paddy husk, and oil-cake water benefits coconut along with kharjura and other palms.
dosage_preparation: Recipe 1: cow/pig/sisumara flesh cooked in water for irrigation (Verse 127). Recipe 2: salt, sesame, honey, and liquor applied externally at night (Verse 129). Recipe 3: alkaline water, barley flour, paddy-husk water, pulse soup for pot-sized fruits (Verse 130). Recipe 4: white mustard water, barley, paddy husk, oil-cake (Verse 131).

medicinal_uses: Classified as a Jangala-adapted (arid/xerophytic region) tree, suited for warmer and drier climatic conditions.

Classified as Jangala-adapted (arid region). In Dohada practice, irrigated with flesh-and-fish water to promote flowering. As an indicator tree, its presence signals suitability for barley (yava) cultivation.
dosage_preparation: Dohada flowering: irrigate with mamsa (flesh) and fish mixed in water (Verse 158).

medicinal_uses: Classified as a Jangala-adapted (arid region) tree suited for warmer climatic zones.
Classified as Jangala-adapted (arid region). As an indicator tree, its presence (along with Khadira) signals the likelihood of famine (durbhiksa) in the area.
Classified as a Jangala-adapted (arid/xerophytic region) tree suited for warmer and drier climatic conditions.
Classified as a Jangala-adapted (arid region) tree. Listed among plants receiving bark-paste treatment with ghee, honey, ksara, lodhra, and barley for fruit enhancement.
dosage_preparation: Bark-paste treatment: ghee, honey, ksara, lodhra, and yava applied after bark scratching (Verse 139).

medicinal_uses: Classified as Jangala-adapted (arid region). In Dohada tradition, the tree blooms profusely when a beautiful young woman sprays wine from her mouth onto it — this process defines the concept of Dohada (the distinctive desire of a plant). Flowering further enhanced with a recipe of kalaya, jaggery, and snake powder mixed in water with mud. Valued as a garden ornamental tree whose flowers attract honey-bees in clusters. Seeds treated under standard procedure and dried with head-cutting for improved viability.
dosage_preparation: Dohada process: wine sprayed from mouth of a young woman (Verse 151). Flowering enhancement: kalaya, jaggery, snake powder in water-mud mixture (Verse 237). Seed treatment: standard processing followed by head-cutting (sirsa-kuticcaram).

medicinal_uses: Classified as Jangala-adapted (arid region) and part of the auspicious pentad (pancavrksa) of trees. In Dohada tradition, when a beautiful young woman adorned with anklet ornaments and painted with lac-dye touches the tree with her lotus-like feet, it instantaneously responds with pleasure and blooms profusely. In horticultural wonders, soil blended with fragrant asoka buds, when filled around any tree's roots, imparts a most pleasing fragrance to that tree.
dosage_preparation: Dohada: contact with ornament-adorned woman's lac-painted feet (Verse 153). Fragrance transfer: asoka bud-blended soil applied to root area of any tree (Verse 226).

medicinal_uses: Classified as Anupa-adapted (marshy/coastal region). Part of the coconut-allied palm group receiving similar nutritive treatments for fruit enhancement.
dosage_preparation: Treated alongside narikela (coconut) group with animal-flesh water irrigation and nutritive oil-cake solutions (Verses 127, 131).

medicinal_uses: Classified as Anupa-adapted (marshy/riverine region). Also recommended for directional planting in the eastern direction near residences.

Classified as Anupa-adapted (marshy region). Part of the coconut-allied palm group receiving nutritive treatments including white-mustard water, barley, paddy husk, and oil-cake water for enhanced fruit production.
dosage_preparation: Nutritive recipe: white mustard water, yava (barley), tusa (paddy husk), pinyaka (oil-cake) mixed for irrigation (Verse 131).

medicinal_uses: Classified as Anupa-adapted (marshy/coastal region). Part of the coconut-allied palm group receiving animal-flesh-and-water irrigation treatments for fruit enhancement.
dosage_preparation: Treated alongside narikela group with animal-flesh water irrigation (Verse 127).

medicinal_uses: Classified as Anupa-adapted (marshy/riverine region). For high yield, twinning grape vines should be incised and fumigated with specific fish mixed in ghee. Additionally, rooster fecal powder applied to roots and fish oil mixed with water given to roots dramatically increases fruit production. Also referenced in taste-modification recipes: grapes included among ingredients for treating bitter-tasting tree roots to produce sweet fruits.
dosage_preparation: High yield Recipe 1: incision with fumigation of saphri fish in ghee (Verse 124). Recipe 2: rooster fecal powder at roots, fish oil-water to roots (Verse 125). Taste modification: draksa with madhuka flowers, licorice, sugar, yava, and milk-kunapa irrigation for sweetening bitter fruit trees.

medicinal_uses: Classified as Sadharana-adapted (general region, suitable for common climatic conditions). Propagated by seed. Soil quality for planting is compared to the colour of campaka flowers as an ideal indicator.

Listed in general plantation principles. In Dohada tradition, Madhavi (along with karavira and kurantaka) is irrigated with sura (alcoholic liquor) in the evening in a humble manner, promoting beautiful flowering.
dosage_preparation: Dohada flowering: irrigate with sura (liquor) in evening (Verse 156).

medicinal_uses: Listed among plants in general plantation principles. Propagated by seed.

Classified as Sadharana-adapted (general region). Listed among restricted campus trees that should not be planted in residential compounds. Fruit-enhancement treatment involves irrigation with flesh mixed in water for best quality fruits.
dosage_preparation: Fruit enhancement: flesh of pherava mixed in water for irrigation (Verse 144).

medicinal_uses: Comprehensive fruit-enhancement recipe involving milk, flesh, fish, cow dung, rice, sesame-seed cake, and water applied to trees. Produces large, tasty, pulpy fruits resembling pots in size, so heavy that the tree bends under their weight.
dosage_preparation: Recipe: milk, flesh, fish, gomaya, sali rice, tila khali, and water mixed and applied (Verse 143).

medicinal_uses: Seeds are naturally curved and should be sown in their standing (curved) position. Fruit-enhancement recipe uses vidanga, masa, sesame, mustard, bilva, turmeric, and rabbit flesh mixed in water for irrigation, supplemented by pasting and fumigation treatments. Produces abundant large, juicy fruits that cause branches to bend with weight.
dosage_preparation: Seed sowing: sow in curved standing position (Section 8). Fruit enhancement: vidanga, masa, tila, sarsapa, bilva, haridra, and sataka mamsa in water for irrigation with pasting and fumigation (Verse 144).

medicinal_uses: Seeds sown for propagation; seedlings may also be planted. In horticultural wonders, stems of trapusa (cucumber) and kusmanda are pasted with honey and ghee, tied together with grass rope, covered with cow dung, and allowed to unite. When cut and grown, the resulting plant produces cucumber fruit in the size of kusmanda (ash gourd). Also used as a host for nimba seed grafting. Seedless fruit production possible by treating seeds with boar oil.
dosage_preparation: Grafting with trapusa: stems pasted with honey and ghee, tied, covered with gomaya, united, then cut and grown (Verses 265-266). Nimba grafting: nimba seed inserted in unripe kusmanda, sealed with ghee-honey, extracted after ripening (Verse 267). Seedless: seeds treated with boar oil (Verse 227).

medicinal_uses: Propagated by semi-matured stem cuttings taken from the lower portion of branches. Best propagated during the month of Kartika (post-monsoon). After planting, should be irrigated constantly for two months, keeping the propagated material nearly submerged in water. Transplanted to appropriate beds when leaves appear within two months.
dosage_preparation: Propagation: semi-matured lower stem cuttings, planted in Kartika month, kept submerged for two months, transplanted when leaves appear (Section 8, page 48).

medicinal_uses: Sown as intercropping on fertile cultivated land alongside other vegetable plants. In horticultural wonders, grafted with kusmanda by pasting stems with honey and ghee, tying together, and covering with cow dung — the united plant produces cucumber-sized fruits in ash-gourd proportions. Listed among vegetable plants receiving pig-bone fumigation during summer for enhanced production.
dosage_preparation: Grafting with kusmanda: stems pasted with honey/ghee, tied with grass rope, covered with gomaya (Verses 265-266). Vegetable fumigation: pig-bone fumigation during summer (Verse 159).

medicinal_uses: May be grown carefully on fertile and cultivated land along with maruvaka and damanaka.
Grown on fertile cultivated land. Special sowing procedure: seeds mixed with soil, irrigated with a solution of cow dung dissolved in water, then gradually watered. Best planted during Kartika month.
dosage_preparation: Sowing: seeds mixed with soil (mrda), irrigated with gomaya-water solution, watered gradually (Section 8).

medicinal_uses: Propagated by tuber. Planting haridra within the house may cause loss of progeny and wealth according to ancient authorities. Its powder (haridra curna) used as fumigation ingredient for plant treatments, including conversion of tamarind into a climber. Also used in colour-change recipes for plant fruits and flowers. Ingredient in irrigation formulations for nagarangaka fruit enhancement.
dosage_preparation: Fumigation: haridra curna for tamarind treatment (Verse 241). Colour change: part of recipes with lodhra, triphala, tila, and yastimadhu for golden-coloured fruits (Verse 236). Fruit recipes: ingredient in nagarangaka treatment (Verse 144).

medicinal_uses: Known as Krmisatru (enemy of worms), extensively used as an insecticidal agent in plant care. Seed/fruit powder applied in fumigation of seeds along with mustard. Used in seed-viability treatments: seeds are treated with honey and vidanga powder after cow-dung rubbing. Combined with ghee and curd-water for vidagdhi fumigation of plants. Ingredient in root-paste for transplanting seedlings (with honey, botanicals, and ghee). Also part of nagarangaka and kapittha fruit-enhancement recipes.
dosage_preparation: Seed fumigation: sarsapa with vidanga fumes (Verse 53). Seed viability: honey and vidanga powder treatment (Verse 54). Transplanting paste: honey, botanicals (mrsala), ghee, and vidanga powder on roots (Section 8). Vidagdhi fumigation: vidanga, ghee, curd-water, mustard, honey (Verse 122).

medicinal_uses: Best planted during the month of Caitra (spring). Listed among vegetable-fruit plants receiving pig-bone fumigation during summer season for enhanced production.
dosage_preparation: Vegetable fumigation: pig-bone fumigation during summer (Verse 159).

medicinal_uses: Part of the auspicious pentad (pancavrksa) of trees. As an indicator tree in hydrology, its presence signals suitability for cultivating mudga (green gram) crops.
Recommended for directional planting in the eastern direction near residences. Listed among trees receiving bark-paste treatment with ghee, honey, ksara, lodhra, and barley for fruit enhancement.
dosage_preparation: Bark-paste treatment: ghee, honey, ksara, lodhra, and yava applied after bark scratching (Verse 139).

medicinal_uses: Recommended for directional planting in the southern direction near residences.

One of the most frequently referenced plants in the text, used ubiquitously in preparations for plant care. Sesame is a key ingredient in seed-treatment paste (with brhati, ash, and mustard), insecticidal paste (sesame-cake with vidanga), fumigation formulations, multiple fruit-enhancement recipes (for coconut, pomegranate, nagarangaka, and others), bark-paste treatments, and colour-change preparations for fruits and flowers. Sesame oil (tila taila) and sesame-seed cake (tila khali) used extensively in plant nutrition.
dosage_preparation: Seed paste: tila with brhati, bhasma, sarsapa for seed coating (Verse 54). Insecticidal: tila khali with vidanga (Verse 101). Fumigation: tila, madhu, yava for 12-day continuous fumigation (Verse 139). Bark paste: with ghee, honey, lodhra, yava (Verse 139). Coconut recipe: saphri fish with tila powder (Verse 128). Colour change: with yava, kinkuka, manjistha for red fruits (Verse 234).
classical_citations: Vrksayurveda of Surapala, Verses 54, 101, 128, 134, 139, 234, 236; used throughout Sections 7-19

medicinal_uses: Best planted during Phalguna month (spring). Fruit yield enhanced by applying fire from dry-grass fuel during Phalguna, followed by spraying with shalika-water during Caitra. Seedless fruit production achieved by treating seeds with boar oil before sowing and irrigating with the same oil-water mixture.
dosage_preparation: Fruit yield: fire from dry grass (trnamala) in Phalguna, shalika-water spray in Caitra (Verse 160). Seedless production: seeds impregnated with boar oil, irrigated with same oil-water (Verse 227).

medicinal_uses: Best planted during Asvina month (autumn). Seedless brinjal fruits can be produced by treating seeds with boar oil (majja/vasa of krodi) before sowing, then irrigating with the same oil-water mixture.
dosage_preparation: Seedless production: seeds impregnated with boar oil, sown in land, irrigated with same oil-water mixture (Verse 227).

medicinal_uses: A key component of Triphala (with Bibhitaka and Amalaki), one of the most important formulations in the text. Triphala used extensively for fumigation and irrigation of fruit trees including panasa and dadima. Haritaki individually used in medicated irrigation mixed with honey, mustard, ghee, and nicula leaves. Part of flower colour-change recipes: mixed with kinkuka and other ingredients, irrigation with this decoction transforms white flowers to golden colour. Also used in treatment of Pitta diseases in plants as part of kvatha (decoction) with licorice.
dosage_preparation: Medicated irrigation: haritaki with honey, sarsapa, ghee, and nicula leaves (Verse 121). Triphala fumigation: haritaki, bibhitaka, amalaki powder mixed with ghee for dadima (Verse 134). Colour change: with kinkuka, karpisal, manjistha, lodhra for golden flowers (Verse 231). Pitta kvatha: with yastimadhu for plant pitta diseases.

medicinal_uses: Part of Triphala (with Haritaki and Amalaki), used extensively in plant fumigation and irrigation for fruit enhancement. Listed among vegetable-fruit plants receiving pig-bone fumigation during summer. As a hydrology indicator tree, the presence of an ant-hill adjacent to Vibhitaka signals underground water at a depth of four-and-a-half men's length.
dosage_preparation: Triphala: combined with haritaki and amalaki for fumigation and irrigation treatments. Vegetable fumigation: pig-bone fumigation during summer (Verse 159).

medicinal_uses: Licorice used in multiple plant-care formulations. In fruit enhancement, combined with sesame, honey, and kumpa for irrigating panasa (jackfruit) roots to produce bigger, ambrosia-tasting fruits. In disease treatment, part of kvatha (decoction) with haritaki for treating Pitta diseases of plants. Key ingredient in taste-modification recipes: vidanga, yastimadhu, yava, milk, and jaggery paste applied to roots of bitter-tasting trees to produce sweet fruits. Also used in fruit preservation: pills made from lotus, madhuka flowers, utpala, honey, sugar candy, and yastimadhu placed at tree roots prolong fruit-bearing duration.
dosage_preparation: Panasa root treatment: yastimadhu, tila, honey, kumpa for irrigating roots (Verse 138). Pitta treatment kvatha: with haritaki. Taste modification: vidanga, yastimadhu, yava, milk, guda paste on roots (Verse 230). Fruit preservation: pills with madhuka, utpala, honey, sugar, yastimadhu in root pit (Verse 228).

medicinal_uses: Propagated by seed and stem methods, and by branch-layering (bending down branches) with cow-dung manure and two-month irrigation until leaves appear, then incision at middle. In Dohada tradition, irrigated with sura (liquor) in the evening along with Madhavi and Kurantaka for beautiful flowering. In horticultural wonders, converted from shrub to climber by planting in a large broken pot filled with cow flesh and earth, provided with cow-dung nutrition, then transplanted into pit filled with cow bones, well-burnt ash, and water. Stems grafted with dadima for unique modifications.
dosage_preparation: Branch-layering: cow-dung manure, 2-month irrigation, incision when leaves appear. Dohada: sura irrigation in evening (Verse 156). Shrub-to-climber: planted in pot with cow flesh and earth, gomaya nutrition, transplanted to pit with bones/ash/water (Verse 240). Grafting: stems tied with dadima stems (Verse 250).

medicinal_uses: Treated with behni (paddy variety) and masa mixed in water for fruit enhancement. As a hydrology indicator tree, its presence signals suitability for growing crops maturing in 60-day periods.
dosage_preparation: Fruit treatment: behni and masa mixed in water for irrigation (Verse 142).

medicinal_uses: Used in insect-elimination paste for treating plant infestations. Combined with kustha (Saussurea lappa) and other ingredients to prepare formulations applied to affected plant parts for removing harmful insects and micro-organisms.
dosage_preparation: Insect paste: combined with kustha and other plant materials, applied to affected parts (Section 17).

medicinal_uses: Used in insect treatments for plants and in the elaborate kapittha (wood apple) seed-purification process. Roots of vaca along with dhatri, abhaya, and other plant roots are boiled in milk as part of the kapittha seed treatment that transforms the tree into a climber form.
dosage_preparation: Kapittha processing: vaca roots boiled with dhatri, abhaya, and other roots in the milk used for kapittha seed treatment (Verses 242-244). Insect treatment: combined with other plant materials (Section 17).

medicinal_uses: Used in insect-elimination paste for treating plant infestations. Combined with ativisa (Aconitum heterophyllum) and other ingredients to prepare formulations applied to affected plant parts.
dosage_preparation: Insect paste: combined with ativisa and other materials, applied to affected plant parts (Section 17).

medicinal_uses: Used in colour-change recipes for plant flowers and fruits. When combined with darada (hingula), milk, and other minerals, and pasted over tree roots, transforms white flowers to golden colour. Also part of recipes for producing red-coloured fruits when combined with yava, kinkuka, nisa, and tila.
dosage_preparation: Golden flowers: manjistha, darada, milk, pidavana pasted on roots (Verse 232). Red fruits: with yava, kinkuka, nisa, tila ground in water for paste and irrigation (Verse 234). White-to-golden: with haritaki, kinkuka, karpisal, lodhra (Verse 231).

medicinal_uses: Powder used in bark-paste treatment for fruit trees along with yava (barley), applied after bark scratching for fruit enhancement. Also part of colour-change recipes: combined with triphala, tila, yastimadhu, haridra, and nilika for producing golden-coloured fruits. Used with haritaki and other ingredients for transforming white flowers to golden colour.
dosage_preparation: Bark paste: lodhra and yava powder with ghee, honey, ksara applied to scratched bark (Verse 139). Golden fruit colour: with triphala, tila, yastimadhu, haridra, nilika as powder/spray on roots (Verse 236). Golden flowers: with haritaki, kinkuka, karpisal, manjistha for decoction irrigation (Verse 231).

medicinal_uses: Listed among restricted campus trees not suitable for planting in residential compounds. Bark used in wound-healing paste for damaged plants. As a hydrology indicator tree, its presence signals rainfall (vrsti). Also referenced in fumigation formulations where its flower (Partha) is used as an ingredient.
dosage_preparation: Wound paste: arjuna bark combined with other barks, applied to tree wounds (Section 17). Fumigation ingredient: arjuna flower (Partha) in plant fumigation powder (Verse 119).

medicinal_uses: Barley is one of the most frequently used ingredients in plant-care formulations. Barley flour (yavacurna) used in coconut fruit-enhancement recipes with alkaline water and paddy husk. Part of bark-paste treatment (with lodhra) for fruit trees. Ingredient in plant disease treatments including anaemia (paleness) cures and sterility remedies for non-fruiting trees. Used in kapittha seed-processing irrigation (with masa, tila, honey, and fish-water). Key ingredient in 12-day continuous fumigation with sesame and honey for fruit trees. Part of colour-change recipes for producing red-coloured fruits.
dosage_preparation: Coconut recipe: yavacurna with alkaline water, paddy husk, pulse soup (Verse 130). Bark paste: with lodhra, ghee, honey, ksara (Verse 139). Fumigation: yava, tila, madhu for 12 days (Verse 139). Kapittha irrigation: with masa, tila, honey, fish-water (Verses 242-244). Red fruits: with kinkuka, manjistha, nisa, tila (Verse 234).

medicinal_uses: Used in treatment of trees damaged by thunderbolts (lightning strikes). Part of restorative formulations applied to severely damaged trees to aid recovery.
dosage_preparation: Thunderbolt-damage treatment: combined with other plant materials in restorative formulations (Section 17).

medicinal_uses: Listed among restricted campus trees not suitable for planting in residential compounds. In horticultural wonders, castor seeds treated with pig fat (kokara vasa) and sown in suitable land reportedly produce fruits resembling karkati (a type of melon/cucumber) rather than typical castor fruits. Seeds also given special wrapping treatment with jaggery solution and heating for three days to enhance viability.
dosage_preparation: Habit modification: seeds treated with pig fat, sown in land (Verse 239). Seed viability: seeds wrapped with plaster and jaggery solution, heated for 3 days (Section 7).

medicinal_uses: In horticultural wonders, the tamarind tree can be converted from its normal tree form into a climber by treating it with a recipe of Triphala powder, sesame, barley, and masa mixed in water, followed by fumigation with turmeric powder. The transformed plant continues flowering regularly.
dosage_preparation: Tree-to-climber conversion: irrigate with triphala powder, tila, yava, masa in water; fumigate with haridra curna (Verse 241).

medicinal_uses: Listed among restricted campus trees not suitable for planting in residential compounds. As a hydrology indicator, Kancana (Kovidara) growing near specific landmarks indicates the presence of underground water sources.

Oil extracted from ankola seeds extensively used for seed-impregnation treatments. Seeds soaked in ankola oil (combined with fish, sisumara, and crocodile oils) and dried produce plants that flower and fruit simultaneously. Ankola fruit juice also used in mango fruit-enhancement recipe along with ghee, honey, and animal fat. Seeds soaked in ankola oil seven times and dried produce rapid germination when irrigated with coconut water.
dosage_preparation: Seed impregnation: seeds soaked in ankola oil, dried, sown, irrigated with coconut water for rapid germination (Verses 259-263). Mango enhancement: ankola fruit juice with ghee, honey, animal fat in water for root treatment (Verse 126).

medicinal_uses: Propagated by seed and stem methods. In Dohada tradition, mallika responds to gentle warming with fire produced from burning grasses, which promotes profuse flowering that attracts honey-bees constantly.
dosage_preparation: Dohada flowering: light warming with fire from grass fuel (Verse 157).

medicinal_uses: Listed among restricted campus trees that should not be planted in residential compounds. Recommended for directional planting in the southern direction. Propagated by seed. Listed among trees receiving bark-paste treatment with ghee, honey, ksara, lodhra, and barley for fruit enhancement. As a hydrology indicator, the presence of an ant-hill adjacent to Badara signals ground water in the western direction at three hands' distance and three men's depth.
dosage_preparation: Bark-paste treatment: ghee, honey, ksara, lodhra, yava applied after bark scratching (Verse 139).

medicinal_uses: Classified among Anupa-adapted (marshy region) plants. Flowering enhanced by irrigating with aromatic decoction prepared from eladi gandha (aromatic herbal oils) mixed with flesh-water. Alternatively, irrigation with honey-water and other waste-liquid mixtures promotes high flower yield.
dosage_preparation: Flowering Recipe 1: decoction of eladi gandha aromatics in flesh-water for irrigation (Verse 150). Recipe 2: honey-water (maksha jala) irrigation.

medicinal_uses: Propagated by seed-tuber method. Root rhizomes of different lotus varieties extracted, tied together with thread, pasted with ghee and honey, then planted — resulting in blooming of multiple flower colours on a single plant. Seeds used in fruit-preservation pills combined with madhuka flowers, utpala, honey, sugar candy, and yastimadhu. Featured prominently in garden pond design alongside nilotpala (blue lotus). Lotus seed treatment with ajagara skin and mud produces lotus flower gardens.
dosage_preparation: Multi-colour experiment: rhizomes of different varieties tied, pasted with ghee-honey, planted (Verse 249). Preservation pills: kamala, madhuka, utpala, honey, sugar, yastimadhu in root pit (Verse 228). Garden production: seeds in ajagara skin, sown in mud, irrigated with flesh-water (Verse 269).

medicinal_uses: Used as an ingredient in decoction (along with jambu leaves and sura liquor) for irrigating all flowering plants to promote maximum bloom and flower yield.
dosage_preparation: Flowering decoction: mustaka with kosira, jambu leaves in sura (liquor) for irrigation of flowering plants (Verse 149).

medicinal_uses: In Dohada tradition, Patala tree should be irrigated with milk mixed with water to promote profuse flowering that attracts honey-bees. As a hydrology indicator tree, its presence signals availability of cows in the area.
dosage_preparation: Dohada flowering: irrigate with milk-water mixture (Verse 157).

medicinal_uses: Listed among tuber-propagated plants in the plant classification system.
Listed among seed-tuber propagated plants in the plant classification system.

Referenced in the spiritual merit section: planting along with dadima, rambha, priyala, and panasa prevents sorrow for seven births. Seeds treated under standard processing and dried with head-cutting for improved viability. Recommended for planting during Sravana month.
dosage_preparation: Seed treatment: standard processing followed by head-cutting (sirsa-kuticcaram) for viability (Section 7).

medicinal_uses: Planting Nila within the house may cause loss of progeny and wealth. If such plants grow naturally on their own within the house, they are considered harmful. This is the view of well-versed authorities.
Sacred grass referenced in hydrology section: land having growth of munja, kusa (darbha), and other grasses with blue soil keeps underground water sources that provide tasty water in abundance.
Referenced in hydrology section: land with growth of munja reed, kusa grass, and blue soil indicates underground water sources providing tasty water in abundance.
Listed among vegetable-fruit plants receiving pig-bone fumigation during summer for enhanced production.
dosage_preparation: Vegetable fumigation: pig-bone fumigation during summer (Verse 159).

medicinal_uses: Leaves and roots used for providing manure (nutrition) to Madhuka trees, contributing to the production of camphor-fragrant flowers.
dosage_preparation: Manure: leaves and roots of Jaalini given as samskrta (nutrition) to madhuka tree (Verse 146).

medicinal_uses: Paddy referenced extensively throughout the text. Paddy husk (tusa) used in multiple formulations: coconut fruit enhancement, fumigation recipes, and soil-covering treatments. Rice-boiled watery part (manda) used for irrigating certain plants for high fruit yield. Paddy-husk ash (tusa bhasma) combined with cow dung as banana manure. Rope made from rice straw used in stem-cutting propagation.
dosage_preparation: Coconut recipe: paddy husk with alkaline water, barley flour, pulse soup (Verse 130). Banana manure: tusa bhasma with gomaya (Verse 141). Rice-water irrigation: tandula manda for high fruit yield (Verse 159).
classical_citations: Vrksayurveda of Surapala, Verses 130, 141, 159; used throughout
